provisions.title: Guidelines on Recovery Planning
provisions.text: Banks should develop and maintain recovery plans that identify options to restore financial strength and viability when the bank comes under severe stress. Recovery plans should include: governance arrangements (roles and responsibilities, escalation procedures, activation triggers); recovery options (capital restoration, asset disposal, cost reduction, business line restructuring); assessment of recovery capacity (quantitative analysis of the capital and liquidity impact of each option); scenario analysis (severe but plausible scenarios including idiosyncratic, market-wide, and combined scenarios); and communication plan (engagement with supervisors, counterparties, and the public).

provisions.title: Recovery Indicators and Triggers
provisions.text: Recovery plans should include a framework of indicators (early warning signals) that would prompt consideration of recovery actions. Indicators should cover: capital indicators (CET1 ratio, leverage ratio, total capital ratio); liquidity indicators (LCR, NSFR, intraday liquidity); profitability indicators (return on assets, net interest margin); asset quality indicators (NPL ratio, provision coverage ratio); and market-based indicators (CDS spreads, share price, credit rating). Indicators should be calibrated with quantitative thresholds at different levels (monitoring, preparatory, activation).

provisions.title: Management of Related Party Exposures
provisions.text: Banks should manage exposures to related parties on an arm's length basis and subject them to appropriate limits and controls. Related parties include: the bank's subsidiaries, affiliates, major shareholders, board members, senior management, and their close family members. Banks should: establish a policy on related party transactions approved by the board; ensure that transactions are conducted at arm's length; set aggregate limits on related party exposures; require board approval for material transactions; and disclose related party exposures. Supervisors should have the authority to require deduction of excessive related party exposures from capital.

provisions.title: Country and Transfer Risk Management
provisions.text: Banks with international exposures should have adequate policies and processes for managing country risk and transfer risk. Country risk encompasses all exposures that may be adversely affected by conditions in a particular country. Transfer risk is the risk that a borrower will be unable to convert local currency into foreign exchange to make cross-border debt service payments. Banks should: identify and assess country risk exposures; set country limits; monitor country risk developments; maintain adequate provisions for country risk; and stress test their international exposures against severe country risk scenarios.

provisions.title: Internal Audit Function in Banks
provisions.text: Banks should have an independent internal audit function that provides the board with assurance on the adequacy and effectiveness of risk management, internal controls, and governance. The internal audit function should: have a charter approved by the board defining its mandate, independence, and reporting lines; report directly to the board audit committee; have unrestricted access to all activities, records, and personnel; follow recognised internal audit standards (IIA Standards); cover all significant activities and processes including risk management, compliance, and financial reporting; and employ a risk-based audit methodology.

provisions.title: Compliance Function in Banks
provisions.text: Banks should have an independent compliance function that identifies, assesses, advises on, monitors, and reports on the bank's compliance risk. The compliance function should: have a formal status within the bank; be headed by a chief compliance officer with sufficient seniority; have adequate resources and staff with appropriate qualifications; have the authority to communicate directly with the board and senior management; monitor compliance with laws, regulations, and internal policies; provide compliance training; and maintain a compliance risk assessment and monitoring programme. The compliance function should cover all areas including AML/CFT, data protection, market conduct, and sanctions.

provisions.title: Supervisory Guidelines on Expected Credit Losses
provisions.text: The Basel Committee has published guidance on credit loss accounting under the expected credit loss (ECL) model (IFRS 9 / CECL). Key supervisory expectations include: credit risk assessment practices should be aligned with accounting frameworks; banks should maintain robust credit risk rating processes; banks should have adequate data, systems, and processes to measure ECL; management judgement (overlays) should be well-documented and subject to governance; and supervisors should assess the adequacy of ECL provisions. The guidance also addresses the interaction between accounting provisions and regulatory capital (transitional arrangements for the impact of IFRS 9 on CET1).

provisions.title: Basel/IOSCO/IAIS Consumer Protection in Financial Services
provisions.text: International standard-setting bodies have endorsed principles for consumer protection in financial services. Key principles include: legal and regulatory framework for consumer protection; role of oversight bodies; equitable and fair treatment of consumers; disclosure and transparency; responsible business conduct; protection of consumer assets; protection of consumer data and privacy; complaints handling and redress; competition; and financial education and awareness. These principles apply to banking, securities, and insurance sectors and should be proportionately implemented.

provisions.title: FSB Assessment of De-Risking
provisions.text: The FSB has assessed the extent, drivers, and effects of the withdrawal of correspondent banking relationships (de-risking). Key findings include: the number of active correspondent banking relationships has declined by approximately 20% since 2011; de-risking has disproportionately affected small and developing countries, money transfer operators, and charitable organisations; drivers include AML/CFT compliance costs, regulatory uncertainty, and profitability concerns; and de-risking can create financial exclusion risks and drive transactions into less transparent channels. The FSB recommends actions to address de-risking including: clarifying regulatory expectations; enhancing due diligence practices; and promoting LEI adoption.

provisions.title: FSB Key Attributes Application to Insurance
provisions.text: The FSB has published guidance on the application of the Key Attributes of Effective Resolution Regimes to the insurance sector. Key elements include: resolution powers for insurers (run-off, portfolio transfer, bridge institution, bail-in); policyholder protection in resolution (continuity of insurance coverage, priority of policyholder claims); triggers for resolution (balance sheet insolvency, inability to pay claims, loss of confidence); cross-border cooperation (crisis management groups for internationally active insurance groups); and the interaction between resolution and policyholder protection schemes.

provisions.title: FSB Minimum Haircut Standards for SFTs
provisions.text: The FSB has established a framework of minimum haircuts on non-centrally cleared securities financing transactions (SFTs). Minimum haircuts apply to transactions where financing against collateral other than government securities is provided to non-banks. Numerical haircut floors vary by collateral type: corporate bonds and other eligible securities: 0.5-6% depending on maturity and credit quality; equities: 6%; and other assets: 10%. The minimum haircut framework aims to limit the build-up of excessive leverage outside the banking system and reduce the procyclicality of secured lending.

provisions.title: FSB Toolkit on Reducing Misconduct Risk
provisions.text: The FSB has published a toolkit for firms and supervisors to reduce misconduct risk. The toolkit covers three pillars: governance and culture (setting expectations, accountability, tone from the top); incentive structures (linking compensation to conduct outcomes, use of malus and clawback, whistleblowing mechanisms); and information sharing (reference-checking of employees, sharing information on dismissals for misconduct, cross-firm and cross-border cooperation). The toolkit recognises that culture is difficult to regulate directly but can be influenced through governance frameworks, supervision, and enforcement.

provisions.title: IOSCO Standards for Securities-Based Crowdfunding
provisions.text: IOSCO has assessed the regulation of securities-based crowdfunding platforms. Key regulatory considerations include: platform registration and authorisation requirements; investor protection measures (investment limits, risk warnings, cooling-off periods); issuer disclosure requirements (business plan, financial statements, risk factors); due diligence obligations of platforms; conflict of interest management (prohibition on platforms investing in offerings); custody of client funds (segregation requirements); and secondary market arrangements. IOSCO notes that a proportionate regulatory approach is needed to balance investor protection with facilitating access to capital for small businesses.

provisions.title: IOSCO Requirements for Central Clearing of OTC Derivatives
provisions.text: IOSCO and CPMI have established requirements for the central clearing of standardised OTC derivatives. Key elements include: mandatory clearing obligations for standardised interest rate swaps, credit default swaps, and other product classes designated by national authorities; equivalence and substituted compliance frameworks for cross-border clearing obligations; access to CCPs (fair, open, and risk-based participation criteria); client clearing arrangements (segregation models, portability of positions and collateral); and trade reporting to trade repositories (real-time reporting of OTC derivative transactions to enable regulatory monitoring).

provisions.title: IOSCO Leverage Measures for Investment Funds
provisions.text: IOSCO has published a framework for measuring leverage in investment funds. The framework proposes two complementary metrics: Gross Notional Exposure (GNE) which sums the absolute value of all long and short positions including derivatives; and the adjusted GNE which nets certain hedging and netting arrangements. The framework aims to provide regulators with consistent data on fund leverage for financial stability monitoring. IOSCO recommends that authorities collect leverage data from regulated funds on a regular basis and use the data to identify funds that may pose systemic risks.

provisions.title: CCP Participant Tiering and Indirect Clearing
provisions.text: CPMI-IOSCO has published standards on CCP participation requirements and indirect clearing arrangements. Key elements include: tiered participation structures (direct clearing members, indirect participants, clients of indirect participants); requirements for direct clearing members (capital adequacy, operational capability, default fund contributions); client clearing requirements (segregation of client positions and collateral, portability arrangements); and transparency about the arrangements available to indirect participants. CCPs should ensure that their tiering arrangements do not create undue concentration risk or impede the ability of smaller market participants to access central clearing.

provisions.title: CPMI-IOSCO Public Qualitative Disclosure Standards for FMIs
provisions.text: CPMI-IOSCO requires FMIs to provide comprehensive public disclosure of their rules, governance arrangements, and risk management practices. FMIs should publish a disclosure framework document following the CPMI-IOSCO template, covering all 24 Principles and (where applicable) the 5 Responsibilities. The disclosure should be updated at least every two years or following material changes. FMIs should also publish: quantitative data on a quarterly basis; their rules, policies, and procedures; fee schedules; and information on recovery and wind-down arrangements. Disclosure promotes transparency and enables participants to assess FMI risk management quality.

provisions.title: IADI Guidance on Differential Premium Systems
provisions.text: IADI has published guidance on risk-based differential premium systems for deposit insurance. Key design considerations include: risk indicators (capital adequacy, asset quality, management/governance, earnings, liquidity, sensitivity to market risk - CAMELS framework); weighting of indicators; premium rate calibration (base rate plus risk adjustment); transition and smoothing mechanisms; data sources and quality; and disclosure of the premium methodology. Differential premium systems incentivise sound risk management by rewarding better-managed banks with lower premiums. IADI notes that the premium system should be fair, transparent, and non-distortionary.

provisions.title: IADI Guidance on Mitigating Moral Hazard
provisions.text: IADI has published guidance on mitigating moral hazard in deposit insurance systems. Key measures include: setting coverage limits at appropriate levels (high enough to protect most depositors but not so high as to eliminate market discipline); implementing risk-based differential premiums; maintaining the credibility of the non-coverage of excluded products; ensuring prompt corrective action by supervisors; applying least-cost resolution principles; and requiring coinsurance or deductibles where appropriate. The guidance also addresses the moral hazard implications of temporary increases in coverage during financial crises.

provisions.title: IAIS Guidance on AML/CFT in the Insurance Sector
provisions.text: The IAIS has published guidance on the application of AML/CFT requirements to the insurance sector. Key areas include: risk assessment (identifying ML/TF risks in life insurance, investment-linked products, and premium financing); CDD for insurance customers (policyholder, beneficiary, and beneficial owner identification); simplified CDD for lower-risk products (term life, personal accident, health insurance); enhanced CDD for higher-risk situations (large single premium payments, third-party payments, complex structures); suspicious transaction reporting; and internal controls and compliance. The guidance recognises that life insurance products generally pose higher ML/TF risks than general (non-life) insurance products.

provisions.title: G20 Commitment to Reduce Remittance Costs
provisions.text: The G20 has committed to reducing the global average cost of remittances. The G20 target is to reduce the global average cost of sending remittances to 5% of the transfer amount, with the objective of eliminating remittance corridors with costs above 10%. Key measures include: promoting competition among remittance service providers; improving payment system infrastructure (interoperability, instant payments); enhancing financial literacy among migrants; supporting regulatory frameworks that enable innovation (mobile money, digital remittances); and publishing remittance cost data to promote transparency (World Bank Remittance Prices Worldwide database).

provisions.title: G20 Data Free Flow with Trust
provisions.text: The G20 has endorsed the concept of 'Data Free Flow with Trust' (DFFT), which aims to promote cross-border data flows while addressing privacy, data protection, intellectual property, and security concerns. In the financial sector context, DFFT has implications for: cross-border sharing of supervisory data; cloud computing (data localisation requirements); consolidated supervision of international banking groups; and the operation of global payment systems. The G20 recognises that data localisation requirements can impede the efficiency of global financial services while acknowledging legitimate regulatory concerns about data sovereignty.

provisions.title: FSB TCFD Recommendations Legacy
provisions.text: The FSB established the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) in 2015, which published its recommendations in 2017. The TCFD recommendations covered four areas: governance (board and management oversight of climate risk); strategy (actual and potential impacts on the organisation's business, strategy, and financial planning); risk management (processes for identifying, assessing, and managing climate-related risks); and metrics and targets (metrics and targets used to assess and manage relevant climate-related risks and opportunities). The TCFD was disbanded in 2023 after the ISSB assumed responsibility for monitoring climate-related disclosures under IFRS S2.

provisions.title: FSB Guidance on Cloud Services and Financial Stability
provisions.text: The FSB has assessed the financial stability implications of the growing reliance of financial institutions on cloud computing services. Key concerns include: concentration risk (a small number of cloud providers serving a large portion of the financial sector); cross-border data governance (data residency requirements, regulatory access to data held in cloud); operational resilience (availability, disaster recovery, incident management); data security and privacy (encryption, access controls, multi-tenancy risks); vendor lock-in (portability, exit strategies); and regulatory oversight (ability of supervisors to audit cloud providers, contractual audit rights). The FSB recommends that regulatory frameworks address these risks proportionately.

provisions.title: IOSCO Guidance on Cyber Resilience for Securities Markets
provisions.text: IOSCO has published guidance on cyber resilience for trading venues, market intermediaries, and asset managers. Key areas include: governance (board-level oversight, cyber risk appetite); threat intelligence (participation in information-sharing organisations, threat landscape monitoring); protective controls (network segmentation, access management, encryption); detection capabilities (security operations centre, continuous monitoring, penetration testing); incident response (response plans, communication protocols, forensic capability); recovery (business continuity, data recovery, lessons learned); and regulatory expectations (cyber risk assessment, reporting requirements, supervisory examinations).

provisions.title: Output Floor for Internal Models
provisions.text: The Basel III output floor constrains the capital benefits that banks can derive from the use of internal models. Risk-weighted assets (RWAs) calculated using internal models approaches must not be lower than 72.5% of the RWAs calculated using the standardised approaches. The output floor is applied at the aggregate level (not risk-category level). It is phased in from 50% on 1 January 2023 to 72.5% on 1 January 2028. The output floor reduces the excessive variability of RWAs across banks and ensures a minimum level of capital is maintained even when internal models produce lower risk estimates.

provisions.title: Buffer Usability and Distribution Constraints
provisions.text: Capital buffers are designed to be drawn down in periods of stress and rebuilt during normal times. When a bank's CET1 ratio falls into the buffer range (above minimum requirements but below the combined buffer requirement), distribution constraints apply. The maximum distributable amount (MDA) limits dividends, share buybacks, and bonus payments. The constraints become progressively more restrictive as the capital ratio approaches the minimum requirement. During the COVID-19 pandemic, supervisors clarified that buffers are designed to be used and encouraged banks to draw down buffers to support lending. This revealed concerns about 'buffer paralysis' and stigma effects.

provisions.title: Interest Rate Floor for EVE Calculations
provisions.text: For the calculation of EVE under the six prescribed shock scenarios, cash flows in each currency should be floored at the lower bound of interest rates. The lower bound varies by maturity: for overnight to 1 year, the floor is -100 basis points; for maturities beyond 20 years, the floor is 0 basis points; with linear interpolation in between. The floor prevents economically unrealistic interest rate assumptions in negative rate environments. Banks should use risk-free yield curves appropriate to each currency for discounting purposes.

provisions.title: Prudent Valuation Framework
provisions.text: Banks should apply prudent valuation adjustments (additional valuation adjustments, AVAs) to fair-valued positions in the trading book. AVAs should cover: market price uncertainty (bid-offer spreads for less liquid positions); close-out costs (costs of liquidating concentrated positions); model risk (uncertainty from the use of valuation models); unearned credit spreads (CVA for counterparty credit risk); investing and funding costs; future administrative costs; and early termination costs. The aggregate of all AVAs is deducted from CET1 capital. Banks should have a robust valuation governance framework including independent price verification.

provisions.title: IAIS Guidance on Policyholder Protection Schemes
provisions.text: The IAIS has published guidance on policyholder protection schemes (PPSs), which protect policyholders in the event of insurer insolvency. Key design considerations include: scope of coverage (which lines of business, which policyholders); coverage limits (per policy, per insurer); funding mechanism (ex ante levies on insurers, ex post assessments, or hybrid); governance (independent board, transparent processes); triggering events (insolvency, regulatory intervention, court order); policyholder priority in insolvency (preferential treatment of policyholder claims); and coordination with resolution authorities (facilitating portfolio transfer or run-off management).
